content.text: What It Is

Resource allocation and balancing is the programme management discipline of distributing available human capacity — engineers, data scientists, ML engineers, product managers, and security reviewers — across competing AI initiatives in a way that maximises the throughput and strategic value of the portfolio without exceeding team capacity or concentrating demand on skill bottlenecks.

It is distinct from team-level sprint planning (which assigns work to individuals within a team) and from financial budgeting (which allocates money). Resource allocation operates at the programme level, asking: given the set of approved initiatives and the set of available teams, how should team-weeks of capacity be distributed across those initiatives over the planning horizon? The output is an allocation map — which team is working on which initiative, at what percentage of capacity, and for how long — combined with a capacity health signal that shows where the programme is over-allocated, constrained, or slack.
Why It Matters

AI programmes suffer from a structural resource problem: demand for skilled AI talent (ML engineers, data scientists, LLM prompt engineers, MLOps practitioners) consistently exceeds supply. Without an explicit allocation model, capacity flows to the most politically vocal initiative rather than the highest-priority one. The result is a portfolio where everything is nominally in progress but nothing is moving fast enough to deliver value.
Prevents over-allocation:
teams working at 120% of capacity for more than two sprints experience quality degradation, technical debt accumulation, and attrition — all of which compound the capacity problem.
Surfaces skill gaps early:

the allocation model reveals which initiatives are blocked not by budget but by the absence of a specific skill (e.g., a senior MLOps engineer with Kubernetes expertise). This triggers a hire or contract decision before it becomes a milestone blocker.
Enables portfolio resequencing:

when a new high-scoring initiative is approved and the programme is at capacity, the allocation model identifies which lower-priority initiative should be paused to make room, converting an implicit trade-off into an explicit, documented decision.
Creates financial accountability:

team-week allocations translate directly to cost (team cost per week multiplied by allocation percentage). This makes the cost of initiating a new initiative visible before it begins, not after six months of spend.
Supports scenario planning:
the allocation model can be run against hypothetical scenarios ('what if we hire three ML engineers in Q3?', 'what if we pause Initiative X?') to inform planning decisions with quantified trade-offs.
Benefits
Faster initiative throughput:
when teams work on fewer initiatives at higher focus (e.g., 80% of capacity on one initiative rather than 20% on five), they deliver faster due to reduced context-switching overhead.
Better hiring decisions:
the allocation model identifies skill gaps with precision — not 'we need more engineers' but 'we need two senior data engineers with streaming pipeline experience for Q3'.
Reduced delivery risk:
initiatives that the allocation model shows as under-resourced can have their milestones adjusted proactively rather than discovering the resource gap two weeks before a gate review.
Stakeholder trust:
when a new initiative is approved, stakeholders can see from the allocation model exactly which initiatives it displaced and why. This is more credible than a vague 'it will be prioritised'.
Contractor planning:
the model identifies which future periods will require contractor augmentation, allowing procurement to begin 6–8 weeks before the need rather than urgently at the point of crisis.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Allocation is never perfectly accurate:

teams are not fungible; an ML engineer allocated to a GenAI project is not the same as one allocated to a computer vision project, even if both are 'ML engineers'. Skill granularity in the model must reflect this.
Frequent rebalancing creates instability:

rebalancing allocations too frequently (e.g., every sprint) creates context-switching overhead that exceeds the efficiency gain. A bi-weekly or monthly rebalancing cadence is typically the right trade-off.
Resistance from initiative owners:

owners whose initiatives are de-prioritised in the allocation model resist the outcome, particularly if they have already made commitments to their business stakeholders based on an assumed resource level that the model doesn't support.
Contractor quality variability:
when gaps are filled with contractors, the quality and ramp-up time vary significantly. The allocation model treats all contractor days as equivalent, which they are not.
The model must account for non-delivery capacity:

engineers spend 15–25% of their time on meetings, on-call rotations, training, and maintenance. Failing to account for this overhead produces allocations that look achievable on paper but require 120% utilisation in practice.
How It Works
Resource allocation and balancing operates through a four-step cycle: capacity mapping, demand modelling, allocation optimisation, and rebalancing.

Capacity mapping catalogues all available delivery capacity for the planning horizon. Each team's capacity is expressed in team-weeks, adjusted for leave, public holidays, and non-delivery overhead (the 20% overhead allowance). Skill tags are attached to each team: a data science team of six might have capacity tagged as '3 FTE ML training, 2 FTE inference/MLOps, 1 FTE data analysis'. The capacity map is a matrix with teams on one axis and time periods on the other.

Demand modelling converts approved initiatives' delivery plans into resource demand. Each initiative's delivery plan is decomposed into phases, and each phase is annotated with its resource requirements: which skills, at what team size, for how long. The aggregate of these demand profiles is summed across all initiatives to produce a total demand curve for each skill type.

Allocation optimisation matches supply to demand, starting with the highest-priority initiative and working down the stack until capacity is exhausted. Where demand exceeds supply for a skill type, the programme manager has four options: defer the lower-priority initiative, hire or contract to fill the gap, resequence phases within an initiative to reduce the demand peak, or reduce scope.

Rebalancing occurs on a bi-weekly cadence triggered by the programme sync ceremony. Changes since the last rebalance are reviewed: have any initiatives' resource needs changed? Have any teams' capacity changed (new joiners, leavers, extended leave)? Are any allocations being under-utilised? The rebalancing output is an updated allocation map that is published to all team leads before the next sprint planning cycle.
Architecture and Design

The resource allocation model has three architectural layers: the skills inventory (supply), the demand model (from delivery plans), and the allocation engine (matching supply to demand with priority ordering).

attrs.alt: Resource allocation and capacity model — demand side matched to supply side through allocation engine, producing staffing outcomes

content.text: The skills inventory is a structured record of every team member's skill profile, current allocation status, and availability for new work. It is maintained by the programme manager with input from team leads. For each skill, the inventory records: the number of FTEs available, the experience level (junior/mid/senior), and the percentage of capacity currently allocated to existing work. The availability figure — the portion of each FTE's capacity that can be directed to a new initiative — is the key input to the allocation engine.

The demand model is derived from initiative delivery plans. Each initiative's programme manager provides a resource demand profile: a per-sprint or per-month estimate of required FTEs by skill type and experience level. These profiles are aggregated across all initiatives to produce a total demand curve. The demand curve is compared against the supply curve (from the skills inventory) to identify periods of over-allocation (demand exceeds supply), balanced allocation, or under-utilisation (supply exceeds demand).

The allocation engine applies the portfolio priority order to assign available supply to demand. It is not a sophisticated optimisation algorithm; in most enterprise AI programmes, a simple greedy algorithm (satisfy the highest-priority initiative first, then the next, until capacity is exhausted) produces adequate results. The key constraint the engine enforces is the WIP limit: no team should be allocated to more than two initiatives simultaneously, because the context-switching overhead of three or more concurrent allocations eliminates more productivity than the parallelism gains.
When to Use It
When the portfolio has more approved initiatives than can be staffed simultaneously:
this is the primary trigger. A portfolio with 14 approved initiatives but capacity for 8 requires explicit allocation management.
When quarterly planning produces commitments that teams then fail to meet:
the root cause is usually that commitments were made without checking the allocation model.
When a new initiative needs to be started and the portfolio is at capacity:
the allocation model identifies which running initiative must be paused or descoped to free the required capacity.
When a key team member leaves or goes on extended leave:
the allocation model immediately shows which initiatives are impacted and by how much, enabling rapid resequencing.
When a shared platform or data engineering team is the bottleneck for multiple initiatives:
the allocation model makes this visible and informs the decision to hire, to prioritise, or to accept the sequential rather than parallel delivery of dependent initiatives.
How to Implement
Build the skills inventory:

for each team, document: team size (FTE count), skill tags with experience levels, current project allocation percentages, and leave commitments for the next 12 weeks. This is the supply side of the model.
Collect initiative resource demand profiles:

for each approved initiative, collect a phase-by-phase resource demand profile from the initiative's delivery lead. Express demand in FTE-weeks by skill type. Review profiles for feasibility — demand profiles that assume 100% FTE availability without overhead allowance need correction before entering the model.
Build the allocation model:

a structured spreadsheet with teams on the rows, time periods on the columns, and initiative codes in the cells is sufficient for portfolios under 20 initiatives. For larger portfolios, use a resource management tool (Resource Guru, Forecast, Float) that provides automated over-allocation detection.
Apply the overhead allowance:
multiply each team's raw FTE count by 0.8 (80% effective capacity) to account for meetings, maintenance, on-call, and leave. Never allocate against 100% of raw capacity.
Run the allocation optimisation:

starting with the highest-priority initiative, assign available supply to demand until the initiative is fully staffed or capacity is exhausted. Record which initiatives are fully staffed, partially staffed (with the gap noted), or unstaffed.
Publish and review bi-weekly:
publish the allocation map to all team leads before each sprint planning cycle. Review changes at the programme sync ceremony.

resource_allocation_model:
  planning_horizon: "Q3 2025"  # 13 weeks
  overhead_factor: 0.80         # 20% non-delivery overhead applied to all teams

  teams:
    - id: data-science
      members: 6
      effective_ftes: 4.8  # 6 * 0.80
      skills:
        - skill: ml-training
          ftes: 2.4
          level: senior
        - skill: inference-mlops
          ftes: 1.6
          level: mid
        - skill: data-analysis
          ftes: 0.8
          level: mid

    - id: platform-engineering
      members: 8
      effective_ftes: 6.4
      skills:
        - skill: kubernetes-infra
          ftes: 2.0
          level: senior
        - skill: api-backend
          ftes: 3.2
          level: mid
        - skill: devops-cicd
          ftes: 1.2
          level: mid

  initiatives:
    - id: contract-ai-assistant
      priority: 1
      phase: build
      resource_demand:
        - skill: ml-training
          ftes_required: 1.5
          weeks: 8
        - skill: api-backend
          ftes_required: 1.0
          weeks: 10

    - id: fraud-detection-v2
      priority: 2
      phase: discovery
      resource_demand:
        - skill: ml-training
          ftes_required: 1.0
          weeks: 6
        - skill: data-analysis
          ftes_required: 0.5
          weeks: 6

  allocation_rules:
    max_initiatives_per_team: 2
    min_focus_pct: 40       # No team allocated at < 40% per initiative
    rebalance_cadence: biweekly

  output:
    over_allocated_skills: []
    at_risk_skills:
      - skill: ml-training
        gap_ftes: 1.1
        impacted_initiatives: [fraud-detection-v2]
    recommendations:
      - "Hire 1 senior ML engineer or contract for Q3 to staff fraud-detection-v2"
Examples
Real-World Example

A European bank's AI centre of excellence was running 22 initiatives across four product lines. Every quarter, the planning meeting ended with a commitment list that nobody believed was achievable. The root cause was identified in a capacity audit: the data engineering team of four was the upstream dependency for 18 of the 22 initiatives, but was only ever allocated 40% of its capacity to programme delivery because the other 60% was consumed by production support and maintenance of legacy data pipelines.

The programme introduced an explicit allocation model. The first run revealed that demand for data engineering capacity was 3.2 FTEs across the committed initiatives but effective supply was 1.6 FTEs (4 × 40%). The model identified 11 initiatives that could not proceed on their planned timelines without either additional data engineering capacity or a redesign that reduced their data pipeline dependency.

The programme director used the model output in a 90-minute executive session to make four decisions: pause 3 initiatives in the bottom quartile of the portfolio scoring model; move 2 initiatives' data engineering phases into a sequential queue; approve 1 contractor hire; and fund a 10-week project to migrate 4 legacy pipelines to a self-serve platform that would reduce the data engineering team's maintenance burden by 30%. By Q2 of the following year, effective data engineering capacity for programme delivery had increased to 2.8 FTEs and 15 of the 22 initiatives were delivered on schedule.
Common Mistakes
Allocating against 100% of team capacity:
effective capacity after overhead is 75–80% of headcount. Allocating against 100% guarantees that commitments cannot be met.
Treating skills as homogeneous:
'ML engineer' is not a single skill. An engineer specialising in NLP fine-tuning cannot be substituted for one specialising in computer vision without significant ramp-up time.
Failing to model the 'shared platform team' bottleneck:

platform and data engineering teams that serve all initiatives are chronically over-allocated because each initiative's demand model accounts for only its own needs, not the aggregate across all initiatives.
Rebalancing too frequently:
weekly rebalancing creates constant interruptions to sprint planning. Bi-weekly is the minimum cadence that gives teams stability while allowing the programme to respond to changes.
Keeping allocation decisions informal:

unrecorded allocation changes ('we agreed in a Slack call to move Sam to Initiative X for two sprints') produce an allocation model that diverges from reality within days. All allocation changes must be recorded in the model.
Best Practices
Always publish the allocation map to team leads before sprint planning:
team leads cannot plan sprints accurately without knowing which initiative(s) their team is funded to work on and at what percentage.
Set a maximum of two simultaneous initiatives per team:
the productivity cost of three or more concurrent initiatives consistently exceeds the parallelism benefit.
Model demand at the skill level, not the team level:
'needs 2 data scientists' is insufficient; 'needs 2 data scientists with LLM fine-tuning experience, at senior level' is the specification needed to detect a real gap.
Build a 13-week rolling allocation horizon:
a horizon shorter than 13 weeks (one quarter) is insufficient for hiring and contracting decisions; longer than 13 weeks, the demand forecasts become too uncertain to be useful.
Review contractor allocation separately:
contractor availability, quality, and cost structure differ from permanent staff. Track them in a separate allocation layer with a weekly utilisation review.
Use the allocation model proactively in intake scoring:

before approving a new initiative, run the allocation model to determine whether it can be staffed. Approving an initiative that the model shows cannot be staffed is a governance failure, not a delivery failure.
Performance and Scalability

Allocation models scale linearly with portfolio size: a 50-initiative portfolio with 20 teams requires the same model structure as a 10-initiative portfolio with 5 teams, but with correspondingly more data maintenance overhead. At scale, the manual maintenance cost of a spreadsheet model typically exceeds 4 hours per week for the programme manager. This is the trigger point for migrating to a dedicated resource management tool (Resource Guru, Forecast, Kantata) that provides automated conflict detection and utilisation reporting.

The bi-weekly rebalancing ceremony scales well up to approximately 10 team leads. Above 10, the ceremony structure must change: team leads pre-submit their capacity changes asynchronously, and the programme manager reviews the aggregate impact before the sync meeting, bringing only conflicts and over-allocation issues to the group.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Spreadsheet-based allocation models (Google Sheets or Excel) are sufficient for portfolios under 20 initiatives and 8 teams, provided the schema is well-designed with automated over-allocation highlighting using conditional formatting. For larger portfolios, dedicated resource management tools include: Resource Guru (simple, team-level, good for SMEs); Forecast (project-skill-person model, integrates with Jira and GitHub); Float (visual timeline interface, good for agencies and creative teams); Kantata (formerly Mavenlink, enterprise-grade with financial integration); and Microsoft Project (Gantt-centric, deep Microsoft ecosystem integration).

For AI-specific programmes, the allocation model should integrate with the portfolio backlog tool so that approving a new initiative from the intake process automatically triggers an allocation check — the tool computes whether the new initiative can be staffed and alerts the programme director if it cannot.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best For
Explicit allocation model
Objective; auditable; enables scenario planning
Requires maintenance; needs skill-level data
Programmes > 8 teams
Team self-allocation (team chooses)
Empowers teams; reduces overhead
Ignores portfolio priority; uncoordinated
Autonomous team models
Budget-based allocation
Financially integrated; clear accountability
Money ≠ capacity; ignores skill mix
Finance-led programmes
Executive decree
Fast; decisive
Ignores capacity constraints; causes burnout
Crisis situations only
Kanban capacity management
Visual; pull-based; WIP-limited
No forward planning; reactive not proactive
Stable, continuous delivery programmes
Security and Reliability

The allocation model contains commercially sensitive information: team sizes, individual skill profiles, and cost structures. It should be stored in an access-controlled system, visible to the programme management team and team leads, but not to the full delivery team or external stakeholders. Individual skill profiles should be aggregated at the team level before being shared with initiative owners to protect individual privacy.

Reliability of the allocation model depends on the accuracy of the two inputs: the skills inventory and the initiative demand profiles. Both decay over time as people join and leave teams and as initiative scope changes. A reliability discipline requires: a weekly confirmation from each team lead that their capacity record is current; a review of all initiative demand profiles at the start of each quarter; and an automated alert when a team's capacity record has not been updated in more than 14 days.
Mental Model

Resource allocation and balancing is like managing a specialist medical team across multiple operating theatres. The theatre schedule (portfolio of initiatives) specifies which procedures (deliverables) need which surgical specialists (skill types) and for how long. The allocation model is the scheduler: it ensures that the cardiac surgeon is not booked into two operating theatres simultaneously, that the anaesthesiologist's capacity is not exceeded, and that the cleaning and prep time between procedures (sprint transitions and context-switching overhead) is included in the schedule. When a new emergency procedure arrives (a high-priority initiative), the scheduler does not simply add it to the schedule — it identifies which elective procedure can be postponed to make room. The model makes the trade-off explicit rather than hoping the team will somehow do both.
Key Takeaways
Resource allocation distributes team capacity across competing AI initiatives at the programme level — it is distinct from sprint planning (individual level) and financial budgeting (cost level).
Always apply an 80% overhead factor to raw team capacity; allocating against 100% guarantees overcommitment.
Model demand at skill level, not team level — 'ML engineer' is not one skill; NLP specialists and computer vision engineers are not interchangeable.
Set a maximum of two simultaneous initiative allocations per team to limit context-switching overhead.
The allocation model is the authoritative source for intake decisions — new initiatives should not be approved if the model shows they cannot be staffed.
Rebalance bi-weekly to maintain accuracy without creating instability for sprint planning.
Shared platform and data engineering teams are chronic bottlenecks in AI programmes; model their allocation across all initiatives to detect over-commitment before it causes milestone slippage.
